data.title: Earnings scoreboard for financials: 18 of 19 companies see Y/Y growth in earnings
data.description:
Most financial companies from the S&P 500 delivered strong profit performance in the latest earnings season, with 15 out of 19 firms beating EPS estimates while 5 trailed estimates.

On the other hand, based on the revenue, performance was more mixed. 10 companies beat revenue estimates, while 9 companies fell short of estimates.

This structure clearly indicates that profitability remains strong, while top-line growth is uneven across the sector. Of the 19 financial companies of the S&P 500 index, 18 reported Y/Y earnings growth, and 15 saw topline growth.

Here is a summary of recent financial results from top financial stocks:

American Express (AXP) reported a revenue beat and EPS beat, driven by strong card spending and resilient demand from premium customers. Margins held steady, and the company reaffirmed its full-year outlook, signaling continued confidence in consumer strength.

Blackstone (BX) posted a revenue beat with Q1 distributable EPS of $1.36, vs. the consensus of $1.34, increased from $1.75 in Q4 2025 and $1.09 in last year’s Q1. Infrastructure and Tactical Opportunities turned in the strongest returns of the quarter, while its Liquid Credit and Opportunistic Real Estate strategies had negative returns.

Capital One Financial (COF) as Q1 earnings and revenue came in lighter than the Wall Street consensus estimates. The credit card lender continued to integrate Discover's operations into its business.

Nasdaq (NDAQ) reported a revenue beat and EPS beat, supported by strong performance driven by growth at the fintech and index segments, even as expenses continued to swell.

Interactive Brokers (IBKR) reported Q1 revenue of about $1.67B, slightly missing estimates, while EPS of $0.60 came in below or in line with expectations. Trading activity remained strong, but lower regulatory fees and some pressure on commissions weighed on overall results.

Synchrony Financial (SYF) Q1 earnings exceeded the Wall Street consensus on Tuesday as its lower provision for credit losses offset a Q/Q decline in net interest income. It also announced a $6.5B stock repurchase authorization, and the board approved a planned 13% dividend increase to $0.34 per share, beginning in Q3 2026.

Moody's (MCO) Q1 earnings exceeded the Wall Street consensus on strong results in its investor services and analytics divisions. The company reaffirmed its 2026 guidance for revenue growth in the high-single-digit percent range and adjusted EPS of $16.40-$17.00.

Chubb (CB) Q1 GAAP EPS fell short of the average analyst estimate, while net realized losses jumped. Net premiums written increased 10.7% year-over-year to $14.01B.

At the industry level, 3 consumer finance banks, 4 exchanges and data firms, 3 asset management companies, 4 property and casualty insurance names, 2 investment banking and brokerage firms, 1 regional bank, and 2 life insurance companies reported earnings this week.

Among the financial companies that are reporting earnings next week are Mastercard (MA), Robinhood Markets (HOOD), S&P 500 (SPGI), Visa (V), The Allstate Corporation (ALL), American International Group (AIG), Aflac (AFL), and T. Rowe Price Group (TROW).

data.title: Top 8 stories: What happened in crypto market this week & what's next?
data.description:
This week in the cryptocurrency market wasn’t just noise but more like a shift in momentum. Policymakers stepped in with clearer intent, institutions doubled down, and Bitcoin (BTC-USD) quietly touched a ~$79K monthly high.

In this article, we break down what happened in the crypto market this week, while the current global market cap stands at ~$2.6T.

  1. Bipartisan push for crypto rules gains pace: Senator Cynthia Lummis said there’s now bipartisan backing in Congress for a crypto market structure bill. That’s one of the biggest crypto market events because clearer rules could finally reduce uncertainty that’s been holding institutions back, reported Watcher Guru.
  2. BlackRock & Strategy's big bet: BlackRock’s iShares Bitcoin Trust (IBIT) saw $709.76M in inflows this week alone. Meanwhile, Strategy (MSTR) bought 34.16K BTC for ~$2.54B, taking total holdings to 815.06K Bitcoin (BTC-USD).
  3. U.S. military testing Bitcoin network: Admiral Samuel Paparo reportedly confirmed the U.S. military is running a Bitcoin (BTC-USD) node and testing how the Bitcoin network can help secure and protect its systems.
  4. Russia opens the door for crypto in global trade: Russia passed a bill allowing businesses to use crypto for cross-border payments, even under sanctions. Bitcoin (BTC-USD) and Ethereum (ETH-USD) are expected to be the first cryptocurrencies allowed under this system.
  5. South Africa tightens crypto movement rules: South Africa plans strict crypto rules—big transactions must go through approved platforms, cross-border transfers need approval, and foreign holdings must be declared. Breaking rules could lead to asset seizure, up to an R1M fine, or 5 years in jail.
  6. Tether freezes $344M USDT in crackdown: Tether (USDT-USD) froze over $344M USDT in coordination with U.S. authorities and the Office of Foreign Assets Control. It highlights the growing role of stablecoins in compliance frameworks.
  7. Tesla takes a $222M hit on Bitcoin valuation: Tesla (TSLA) Q1 2026 revenue rose 16% YoY to $22.38B, with strong profits and $1.44B free cash flow. The company didn’t buy or sell Bitcoin (BTC-USD), but its digital asset value fell by ~$222M to $786M due to a decline in BTC prices.
  8. Coinbase & Bybit explore tokenized U.S. stocks: Coinbase (COIN) is in talks with Bybit to bring U.S. stocks on-chain, including pre-IPO shares, using tokenization and global distribution. Talks don’t include any investment or U.S. entry for Bybit.

What to Watch Next?

  • Donald Trump speaking at a crypto conference—expect policy signals or market-moving commentary.
  • Federal Reserve meeting on April 28–29.
  • DOJ expected to drop criminal investigation into Fed Chair Jerome Powell.
  • Fed balance sheet update release—key for tracking liquidity direction.

data.title: Notable analyst calls this week: CrowdStrike, AMD and Sandisk among top picks
data.description:
The S&P 500 (SP500) closed in the green on Friday after the week saw a flurry of corporate earnings from Tesla, Intel, and United Airlines.

For the week, Nasdaq (COMP:IND) rose marginally, while Dow (DJI) lost 0.3%.

Wall Street had a slew of upgrades and downgrades from analysts. Here are some of the major calls for the week:

CrowdStrike upgraded on AI tailwinds

KeyBanc Capital Markets upgraded CrowdStrike (CRWD) to Overweight and establish a $525 price target, citing expected tailwinds from artificial intelligence.

The brokerage said it upgraded as it sees “Mythos as a catalyst for accelerating security budgets, the breadth and depth of the Falcon platform well positioned to capitalize on near-term hygiene priorities and long-term runtime defense requirements, strong defensibility to AI-disruption risk, and an incremental competitive advantage from its Glasswing inclusion.

Other short-term positives for CrowdStrike include its Falcon platform, which should get more attention ahead of any risk coming from Anthropic's Mythos model, as well as increased customer engagement, it said.

AMD upgraded on CPU explosion

AMD (AMD) was upgraded at D.A. Davidson, as the investment firm cited a “structural increase” in CPU demand, due to agentic artificial intelligence.

“We are upgrading shares of AMD to Buy from Neutral and raising our price target to $375 from $220 based on a structural increase in CPU demand on top of much improved visibility into AMD's role in the great data center buildout,” analyst Gil Luria wrote in a note to clients. “We believe that given the magnitude of Intel's beat, there is meaningful upside to AMD's estimates, starting with the March quarter which AMD is due to report May 5th.”

“We view Intel's results as a precursor for a huge step-up for AMD's CPU franchise and believe the structural shift towards agentic AI workloads is creating unprecedented demand for server CPUs," Luria added.

Sandisk gets bullish rating at GF Securities ahead of earnings

Sandisk (SNDK) was upgraded to Buy at GF Securities, with the investment firm putting a $1,277 price target on the stock.

“SNDK will report FY3Q26 revenue on April 30, and we expect a strong result and guidance given recent upside surprise from consumer electronics,” analyst Yang Zhou wrote. The brokerage forecast SNDK’s FY3Q revenue at $4.9B and margin at 71.5% with FY4Q26 guidance [greater than] $10B and margin [greater than] 84%.

“We expect another catalyst to be the increase in liabilities, suggesting meaningful down payment of LTAs,” it added.

Morgan Stanley upgraded Phillips 66 (PSX) to Overweight from Equal Weight with a $174 price target, raised from $147, pointing to chemicals as a key differentiator for the refiner and noting increasingly attractive valuation.

Morgan Stanley downgraded Zscaler's (ZS) rating to Equal-weight from Overweight and reduced the price target to $155 from $200, citing growing competition in Secure Access Service Edge, or SASE.

Analysts led by Meta Marshall said that when they upgraded Zscaler to Overweight last year, they did so on the view that the company could expand its platform.

"While we have seen early traction here, with ZDX bookings up 80% Y/Y to $100mm ARR, we have not yet seen similar traction with Red Canary (as the company looks to incorporate capabilities into Zero Trust Exchange), making it difficult to see the platform story really playing out for now. That puts focus squarely on ZIA/ZPA [Zscaler Internet Access/Zscaler Private Access], which are growing in the mid-teens Y/Y, challenging the ability to sustain 20%+ growth rate once Red Canary becomes inorganic in FY27," said Marshall and her team.

data.title: Musk fraud claims against Altman, OpenAI dismissed
data.description:
• A California federal judge on Friday dismissed fraud claims alleged by Elon Musk that OpenAI, the artificial intelligence company he helped found, abandoned its founding mission to operate as a non-profit organization.
  • However, a trial will still start on April 27 to address the Tesla (TSLA) CEO's breach of charitable trust and unjust ​enrichment claims against OpenAI and co-founder Sam Altman.
  • Musk has argued that the company turned its back on its origins when it accepted a multi-billion-dollar investment from Microsoft (MSFT), which currently has a 27% stake, as well as creating a for-profit entity in 2019, after he left the OpenAI board.
  • He is asking for a court order to change OpenAI's status back to a non-profit and also that Altman and company President Greg Brockman lose those positions.
  • Musk is also seeking $150B in damages, according to Reuters. If successful, those proceeds would go to OpenAI's charitable division.

data.title: American Express, Bank of America, Mastercard in losers: week's financials wrap
data.description:
The State Street Financial Sel Sec SPDR ETF (XLF) closed the trading week 1.01% lower at $51.42.

Investors evaluated the latest developments in the Middle East, including a three-week extension of the truce between Israel and Lebanon.

Meanwhile, earnings releases continued to impact stock movements during the week.

Among megacap stocks, American Express (AXP) led the losers, retreating 5.31% to close the week at $314.08.

The credit card company's Q1 earnings and revenue topped Wall Street consensus estimates as its cardholders continued to spend. Network volume and billed business growth increased from the prior quarter's pace.

However, provision for credit losses jumped ~9% from the prior year.

Bank of America (BAC) was the next on the list, falling 3.45% to close at $52.05.

Mastercard (MA) (-3.29% to $504.17) was the next major loser. MA is set to report its quarterly financial results next week.

Goldman Sachs Group (GS) was the only gainer in the category, advancing 0.10% to $926.91. The week saw the banking giant agree to settle a class action lawsuit that accused it of defrauding shareholders about its work for 1MDB, the Malaysian sovereign wealth fund at the center of a multibillion-dollar scandal.

For largecap stocks, Woori Financial (WF) (-9.66% W/W to $67.14) and Nomura Holdings (NMR) (-8.59% W/W to $7.87) led the weekly decliners after posting their quarterly results.

Banco Bilbao Vizcaya Argentaria (BBVA) (-8.30% W/W to $21.87) was the next major loser in the category.

SEI Investments (SEIC) (+11.34% to $88.84) was a notable gainer. The company posted a Q1 EPS and revenue beat this week.

Figure Technology Solutions (FIGR) (-12.33% W/W to $32.15), Sprott (SII) (-11.93% W/W to $130.69), and Banco BBVA Argentina (BBAR) (-11.86% W/W to $14.43) led the midcap losers.

Meanwhile, Washington Trust Bancorp (WASH) (-14.35% W/W to $30.81) and NB Bancorp (NBBK) (-11.91% W/W to $19.82) were the notable smallcap losers, having posted disappointing Q1 earnings and revenue.

BitFuFu (FUFU) was a major decliner, retreating 14.17% W/W to $2.12.

data.title: Senate banking committee vote on Warsh set for April 29
data.description:
• The Senate Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs Committee will vote on whether to advance the nomination of Kevin Warsh as the next Federal Reserve chairman on Wednesday, April 29.
  • The executive session, which will take place at 10a ET on that day, was scheduled late on Friday.
  • "No Republican claiming to care about Fed independence should support moving forward the nomination of Kevin Warsh, who proved in his nomination hearing to be nothing more than President Trump’s sock puppet," committee Ranking Member Elizabeth Warren (D-Mass.) said in a statement.
  • Current Fed Chair Jerome Powell's term ends on May 15.

More on the Federal Reserve

  • Senate banking Democrats want more info from Warsh on his finances

  • Warsh declares Fed policy independence as essential and urges Fed to stay in its lane
  • Trump says he will fire Fed's Powell if he doesn't leave 'on time'
  • Fed officials see higher risk in inflation and labor market, while the Iran war clouds outlook: FOMC minutes

data.title: Earnings Scoreboard: 82% of S&P 500 early reporters top EPS estimates ahead of big tech wave
data.description:
S&P 500 earnings moved into high gear this week, with 91 companies across the financial, healthcare, and industrial sectors providing a vital barometer for the U.S. economy. Strong results, particularly in capital markets and AI-driven infrastructure, have set a high bar as the market prepares for a wave of mega-cap tech reports starting Monday.

Earnings Roundup:

Bottom Line: Out of the 91 companies reporting, 75 exceeded EPS expectations, 13 fell short, and three met estimates. Notably, 77 firms posted year-over-year earnings growth.

Top Line: Performance remained robust on the revenue front, with 66 companies topping estimates while 24 missed. On a year-over-year basis, 80 of the 91 companies reported revenue expansion.

 

 

Let’s take a look at some of the companies that reported earnings this week:

GE Aerospace (GE): Delivered a double-beat ($11.6B rev / $1.86 EPS) with 29% revenue growth; management maintained its 2026 guidance.

Boeing (BA) exceeded Q1 2026 expectations with a smaller loss and higher revenue. The backlog hit $695 billion with 6,100 airplanes. Deliveries rose to 143, increasing revenue by 13% to $9.2 billion. The 737 program produces 42 aircraft monthly, with 737-7 and 737-10 certifications expected in 2026 and deliveries in 2027.

Tesla (TSLA): Surpassed revenue and EPS expectations ($22.39B / $0.41) fueled by energy storage; driven by a surge in services and FSD subscriptions; reaffirmed volume growth targets while raising 2026 capex guidance to over $25B.

Intel (INTC): Posted a strong double-beat ($13.58B / $0.29) and issued upbeat Q2 revenue guidance of $14.3B, driven by AI server CPU demand.

Blackstone (BX): Beat on both lines ($3.62B / $1.36) as AUM reached a record $1.3 trillion; signaled a bullish outlook for AI-linked data center infrastructure.

3M Co. (MMM): Beat on adjusted EPS ($2.14) despite flat $6.0B revenue; reiterated full-year 2026 guidance with an emphasis on margin expansion.

Kinder Morgan (KMI): Topped expectations ($4.83B / $0.48) on robust LNG demand; reiterated full-year 2026 guidance.

Procter & Gamble (PG): Strong double-beat ($21.24B / $1.59); maintained FY2026 outlook while flagging potential tariff-related headwinds.

Philip Morris (PM): Strong double-beat ($10.15B / $1.96) led by smoke-free volumes; updates full-year outlook on ZYN and IQOS momentum.

UnitedHealth (UNH): Beat expectations ($111.72B / $7.23) despite Change Healthcare headwinds. They raised their full-year earnings outlook to over $18.25 per share.

Southwest Airlines (LUV): Missed on both top and bottom line estimates ($7.25B / $0.45),  suspended full-year guidance citing Middle East fuel price volatility.

AT&T (T): Beat on both top and bottom lines ($31.5B / $0.57) behind 5G subscriber gains; reconfirmed FY2026 EPS guidance of $2.25–$2.35.

Earnings next week

Wall Street is bracing for the peak of the first-quarter earnings season, with more than 175 S&P 500 companies, representing over a third of the index’s market cap, scheduled to disclose results next week.

The high-stakes lineup is headlined by the 'Magnificent Seven' tech and communication giants, including Microsoft (MSFT), Alphabet (GOOGL), Meta Platforms (META), and Amazon (AMZN). The healthcare sector will provide a look into pharmaceutical resilience with updates from Eli Lilly (LLY), Merck (MRK), and Bristol Myers Squibb (BMY). Meanwhile, consumer and industrial sentiment will be tested by reports from Coca-Cola (KO), Starbucks (SBUX), and Caterpillar (CAT).

Rounding out the week, the financial and energy sectors will see major updates from payment processors Visa (V) and Mastercard (MA) and oil majors ExxonMobil (XOM) and Chevron (CVX).

More on markets

data.title: What’s next for Apple after Tim Cook’s CEO transition announcement?
data.description:
Apple Inc. (AAPL) announced a major leadership transition on April 21, with Tim Cook stepping down as CEO after a 15-year tenure that saw the company’s stock appreciate more than 10x. John Ternus, currently head of hardware engineering, will assume the CEO role on Sept. 1, while Cook transitions to the position of executive chairman.

Despite the historic nature of this succession, analyst sentiment remains cautiously mixed, with experts weighing the promise of hardware-driven innovation against concerns about elevated valuations and AI strategy execution.

What Do Seeking Alpha Analysts Say About Apple’s Future?

Optimists pointed to Apple’s differentiated AI strategy, which emphasizes on-device inference and privacy-centric edge computing rather than the heavy capital expenditure approach taken by hyperscalers. With 2.5 billion active devices, Apple’s installed base provides a massive platform for localized AI integration without the depreciation costs associated with large-scale data center investments. John Ternus’s deep hardware engineering background is considered a potential catalyst for accelerating product innovation in the AI era.

Pessimists, however, highlighted Apple’s elevated valuation of 32x to 34x forward earnings, which trades at an 18.7% premium to Big Tech peers. Critics pointed to the company’s perceived lack of AI leadership compared to other Magnificent 7 companies, as well as operational risks including rising memory costs, TSMC capacity constraints, and intensifying competition in China.

Here’s a breakdown of what some analysts had to say:

  • JR Research, Investing Group Leader, Rating: Buy: “I see Apple doubling down on hardware and product development to navigate this AI era, while integrating deeper with third party models… Tim Cook will assume the executive chair, helping to maintain its core strategic focus and organizational culture.” - Apple: Tim Cook Hands Over, But This Is Not A Great Reset

  • The Value Portfolio, Investing Group Leader, Rating: Buy: “AAPL’s strategy centers on margin improvements, aggressive share repurchases, and a focused business model, driving strong EPS and net income growth… Recent results show double-digit sales and nearly 20% YoY net income growth, with robust performance across categories and geographies.” - Apple’s New Leadership Preserves The Story

  • Danil Sereda, Investing Group Leader, Rating: Hold: “AAPL’s AI strategy emphasizes on-device inference and privacy-centric edge computing, avoiding heavy CAPEX and leveraging its 2.5 billion active device base… Despite stable EPS growth and fair valuation, I maintain a ‘Hold’ rating, awaiting clearer upside catalysts and post-transition consolidation.” - Apple: Tim Cook Stepping Down As CEO Opens A New Chapter

  • Deep Value Investing, Analyst, Rating: Hold: “The bull case hinges on disciplined AI. The fact that Apple is behind the other Magnificent 7 in the AI race is actually a plus… The company needs to monetize default Siri placement for outside LLM providers instead of funding in-house models or building or renting out data centers.” - Apple: Next CEO Should Rent Out Siri, Not Rebuild It

  • YR Research, Analyst, Rating: Hold: “I upgrade AAPL to ‘Hold’—valuation already reflects its moat and near-term growth, but long-term upside is limited without a new growth catalyst… While Cook’s time at Apple cannot be described as anything less than extraordinary, some investors grew increasingly frustrated.” - Apple: Tim Cook Stepping Down On A High Note (Rating Upgrade)

  • Bill Maurer, Analyst, Rating: Hold: “AAPL maintains strong fundamentals with robust iPhone sales, expected 15% Q2 revenue growth, and significant capital returns… Valuation remains elevated at 31.75x 2026 EPS, trading at an 18.7% premium to Big Tech peers, potentially limiting near-term AAPL upside.” - Apple Enters New Leadership Era

  • Louis Gerard, Analyst, Rating: Sell: “Leadership transition to John Ternus signals a shift toward engineering and product innovation but introduces the risk of multiple compression if AI strategy lags… Apple Inc. is rated a Sell due to its elevated 34x earnings multiple amid strategic uncertainty and AI execution concerns.” - Apple: The CEO Transition Is A Multiple Compression Catalyst

What Do the Quant Ratings Say?

Apple currently carries a Hold rating from Seeking Alpha’s quant system. While the company demonstrates exceptional profitability, its premium valuation and mixed growth outlook present challenges for investors seeking outperformance.

Profitability (A+): Apple continues to demonstrate strong profitability metrics, earning an A-range grade. The company delivered nearly 20% year-over-year net income growth with robust performance across product categories and geographies.

Valuation ((F)): Valuation scores in the D/F range, reflecting the stock’s 32x to 34x forward P/E multiple. AAPL trades at a 10% premium above its five-year average and the highest multiple among Big Tech peers, suggesting limited room for further expansion.

Growth ((D)): Growth metrics present a mixed picture. Consensus projects a 5-year EPS CAGR of 13.44%, which supports the current valuation but may not deliver market-beating returns at the current multiple.

Momentum ((B)): Momentum remains stable, with shares trading near a fair value estimate of $281.1 based on a 30x multiple, representing approximately 3% to 4% upside from current levels.

Earnings Revisions (B+): Q2 guidance calls for 13% to 16% revenue growth with strong margins, providing near-term support. However, tougher comparisons loom for Q3, which analysts view as a more defining quarter for the stock.

What’s the Latest News on Apple?

Apple’s transition comes amid competitive pressure from peers including Microsoft (MSFT), Meta (META), and Alphabet (GOOG). The company guided for approximately 15% Q2 revenue growth, though some investors have grown frustrated with Apple’s conservative approach, including the shuttered Apple Car initiative and what some perceive as a lack of AI leadership.

Operational risks remain a concern for the incoming CEO. Analysts flagged potential TSMC (TSM) N3 capacity constraints and rising memory prices as challenges that could pressure margins in upcoming quarters. Competition in China, Apple’s most important ex-U.S. market, also presents ongoing uncertainty, though recent Q1 performance in the region showed strength.

data.title: Texas Instruments sees bullish views at GF Securities after Q1 results
data.description:
GF Securities (Hong Kong) raised the price target on Texas Instruments' (TXN) stock to $270 from $220 while maintaining its Buy rating after first quarter results beat estimates.

Analysts led by Alicia Xia said that Industrial recovery strengthened, while Automotive stabilized. 

"At the end-market level, the recovery continues to broaden, with strength extending across a wider set of end markets. Industrial grew more than 30% YoY and over 20% QoQ, with the mgmt. highlighting that demand strength has broadened beyond a few resilient verticals to include the long tail of customers," said Xia and her team.

The analysts added that Automotive was up mid-single digits year-over-year and roughly flat quarter-over-quarter, with China weaker while other regions improved, indicating a more uneven recovery profile. Communications Equipment rebounded strongly, while Personal Electronics remained relatively stable, the analysts noted. 

"Overall, industrial appears to be entering a more sustained recovery phase, while automotiveis stabilizing at elevated levels rather than accelerating," the analysts added.

In addition, Xia and her team said that Data Center remained the primary structural growth driver.

Data center revenue grew about 90% year-over-year and over 25% quarter-over-quarter in the first quarter of 2026, marking continued momentum driven by AI-related power demand, the analysts noted.

The analysts said that the management expects Industrial and Data Center to lead growth again in the second quarter, although they remain cautious on the second half given prior false starts in the broader cycle.

Inventory and cash flow trends improved, with inventory levels declining  quarter-over-quarter and capex guidance remaining below recent peaks, supporting a recovery in free cash flow, according to the analysts.

"Management also emphasized that pricing was stable both QoQ and YoY, which is notable given that 1Q26 pricing is usually modestly down. Overall, the mgmt. reinforced a combinationof cyclical recovery in core markets and an increasing contribution from AI-driven demand," said Xia and her team.

data.title: ServiceNow's hybrid pricing gains ground as 50% of new revenue is non-seat-based: CFO
data.description:
ServiceNow's (NOW) hybrid pricing shift has led to 50% of all new business coming from non-seat-based pricing models, according to Chief Financial Officer and President Gina Mastantuono during an interview with Seeking Alpha. 

This is important because a primary reason behind the recent decline of enterprise software stocks stems from concerns over seat-based pricing models. The idea is that as enterprises increase their usage of artificial intelligence tools, efficiencies will improve and headcounts will be reduced or remain flat, thus reducing the number of available seats at an enterprise.

However, despite the rise of AI agents, ServiceNow has not been losing seats.

"AI is unlocking productivity, but seat count remains healthy and continued to grow year over year in Q1," Mastantuono said. "We have a hybrid model that is seat-based first, and as you consume more tokens, fees go in. Over 50% of our new business comes from non-seat-based models. It's not new. We've had this for years, based on infrastructure and services. What's new is the AI consumption model on top of that."

First-quarter results featured 22.1% year-over-year revenue growth and a 2026 outlook that surpassed market expectations. The company's AI ACV is now on track to surpass $1.5B this year, which is a 50% jump from the prior target of $1B. 

ServiceNow shares took a beating after the release. The stock made a historic 19% plummet on Thursday, April 23. Financial analysts linked the drop to a slight decline in the gross margin outlook related to the recent acquisition of Armis. Furthermore, the ongoing conflict in the Middle East created a 75 basis point headwind due to delayed closings of several large, on-premise deals in the region.

"We're looking at short-term margin headwinds here (full-year non-GAAP operating margin to 31.5% from the prior 32%, and full-year free cash flow margin to 35% from 36%)," said Seeking Alpha analyst Oliver Rodzianko. "That's the basic math behind the sell-off, but this is incredibly short-term and misses the fact that the Armis acquisition will only dilute profitability temporarily."

Most investment firms remained upbeat on the stock. BTIG retained its Buy rating but lowered its price target to $150 from $185. Needham also maintained its Buy rating and reduced its price target to $115 from $155. Finally, Stifel reiterated its Buy rating, lowering its target to $120 from $135.

"Zooming out, we expect NOW's numbers to increase as the year progresses," said Stifel analysts Allan Verkhovski and Nick Dannewitz in an investor note. "The company has successfully closed its pending acquisitions, positioning itself to deliver a comprehensive platform with AI, data, security, and governance embedded into every product and package. We look forward to Financial Analyst Day on May 4th to learn more about how this integrated approach can further drive a flywheel of AI consumption on a multi-year basis."

Despite the noise that large language models have the potential to upend traditional enterprise software firms, Mastantuono said AI has been a driving force behind ServiceNow's sustained 20%-plus revenue growth quarter after quarter.

"Our product portfolio continues to outperform," she said. "The number of customers spending on our AI control tower continues to increase. The signal is clear: customers are clearly moving from experimentation to scale … AI structurally increases demand. It does not decrease it. And we don't just sell this platform. We run on it."
